principles.label: The Limits of Language and Silence
principles.summary: There are absolute logical limits to what can be meaningfully expressed in language. Whatever falls outside these boundaries—such as ethics, aesthetics, and metaphysics—must be passed over in silence.
principles.details: Language can only meaningfully picture factual states of affairs. Attempting to articulate the unutterable leads to nonsense. However, what cannot be said is not lost; it is 'shown' or made manifest through the structure of language and life.
principles.representative_quote: What can be said at all can be said clearly; and whereof one cannot speak thereof one must be silent.

principles.label: Philosophy as an Activity of Clarification
principles.summary: Philosophy is not a body of scientific doctrine or a set of theories, but a therapeutic activity aimed at the logical clarification of thoughts.
principles.details: Philosophical problems are not empirical questions to be solved; they are conceptual confusions arising from a failure to understand the logic of our language. The goal is to dissolve these knots and free humanity from neurotic philosophical questing.
principles.representative_quote: Philosophy is not a theory but an activity. A philosophical work consists essentially of elucidations.

principles.label: Meaning as Use
principles.summary: The meaning of a word is not an object it names or a hidden mental state, but its practical use within the context of a language-game.
principles.details: Words do not have fixed, abstract meanings independent of context. Knowing a word's meaning means knowing how to employ it within the shared practices, behaviors, and forms of life of a community.
principles.representative_quote: For a large class of cases of the employment of the word 'meaning'—though not for all—this word can be explained in this way: the meaning of a word is its use in the language.

principles.label: The World as the Totality of Facts
principles.summary: Reality is determined by what is the case—the existence and non-existence of atomic facts—rather than just a collection of isolated objects.
principles.details: Objects only have meaning and reality in their configuration with one another within logical space. The world is determined by these facts and their combinations.
principles.representative_quote: The world is the totality of facts, not of things.

principles.label: The Picture Theory of Propositions
principles.summary: Language and thought represent reality by acting as logical pictures of facts.
principles.details: To represent reality, a proposition must share a logical form with the reality it depicts. Truth or falsity is determined by comparing this logical picture against the actual facts of the world.
principles.representative_quote: The proposition is a picture of reality. The proposition is a model of the reality as we think it is.

principles.label: Philosophy as Work on Oneself
principles.summary: Intellectual achievements and logical cleverness are secondary to moral integrity; the ultimate goal of philosophical work is to settle accounts with oneself.
principles.details: Logic and ethics are two sides of the same coin. When faced with the discrepancy between the world as it is and as it ought to be, the moral task is to seek the source of that discrepancy within rather than trying to meddle with the outside world.
principles.representative_quote: work on philosophy is really more work on oneself.

principles.label: The Independence of the World from the Will
principles.summary: We cannot control external happenings or bend the world to our desires; true mastery comes from renouncing influence over external events.
principles.details: Because the world operates independently of our will, the only way to achieve happiness is to change our internal view of life, altering the limits of our world rather than the facts within it.
principles.representative_quote: I can only make myself independent of the world—and so in a certain sense master it—by renouncing any influence on happenings.

principles.label: The Diversity of Word Functions
principles.summary: The functions of words are vastly diverse, despite their uniform appearance in speech or print.
principles.details: We are often confused by assuming all words work the same way (e.g., naming objects). In reality, words have fundamentally different applications, much like the different tools in a toolbox.
principles.representative_quote: Think of the tools in a tool-box: there is a hammer, pliers, a saw, a screw-driver, a rule, a glue-pot, glue, nails and screws.—The functions of words are as diverse as the functions of these objects.

frameworks.label: Philosophical Therapy
frameworks.summary: A method for dissolving philosophical perplexity by examining how language is actually used, rather than building abstract theories.
frameworks.details: When feeling trapped by a conceptual problem, recognize it as linguistic confusion. Resist the urge to theorize. Instead, 'look and see' how the problematic words are used in ordinary language. Marshall reminders and examples of various language-games to break the illusion of a single, essential meaning, thereby showing 'the fly the way out of the fly-bottle.'
frameworks.representative_quote: The work of the philosopher consists in marshalling reminders for a particular purpose.

frameworks.label: Logical Clarification of Thoughts
frameworks.summary: An analytical approach to identifying nonsense in traditional philosophical musings by translating expressions into strict logical symbolism.
frameworks.details: Recognize that most philosophical questions arise from misunderstanding the logic of language. Translate expressions into a logical symbolism that obeys logical grammar. Identify what can be clearly said (propositions of natural science) versus what can only be shown, and state the senselessness of questions that attempt to speak about the unspeakable.
frameworks.representative_quote: The object of philosophy is the logical clarification of thoughts.

frameworks.label: Language-Games Analysis
frameworks.summary: A framework for dispelling philosophical fog by studying language in primitive, highly circumscribed applications.
frameworks.details: Imagine a primitive use of language (e.g., a builder and an assistant using only 'block', 'pillar', 'slab', 'beam'). Observe how the words are taught through training and practice. Analyze the specific role of the words within the actions they are woven into. Use this simplified model to command a clear view of complex language use.
frameworks.representative_quote: It disperses the fog to study the phenomena of language in primitive kinds of application in which one can command a clear view of the aim and functioning of the words.

mental_models.label: Language-Games
mental_models.summary: Viewing language not as a static, abstract system, but as a diverse collection of rule-governed activities interwoven with human actions and forms of life.
mental_models.details: Words only come alive and have meaning within specific, lived contexts. If a lion could speak, we would not understand it because we do not share its 'form of life'.
mental_models.representative_quote: Here the term "language-game" is meant to bring into prominence the fact that the speaking of language is part of an activity, or of a form of life.

mental_models.label: The Picture Theory of Language
mental_models.summary: The idea that thoughts and propositions act as isomorphic pictures or models of reality.
mental_models.details: A picture models reality because its elements are arranged in a specific structure that corresponds to the arrangement of objects in reality. They share a 'logical form'.
mental_models.representative_quote: We make to ourselves pictures of facts.

mental_models.label: The Tool-box
mental_models.summary: A lens for viewing words not as uniform labels for things, but as diverse instruments with entirely different functions.
mental_models.details: Just as a toolbox contains items with entirely different functions (a hammer modifies a nail's position, a rule modifies our knowledge of length), a language contains words with entirely different applications.
mental_models.representative_quote: The functions of words are as diverse as the functions of these objects.

mental_models.label: Family Resemblance
mental_models.summary: A way to understand how concepts apply to multiple things without sharing a single essential feature, but rather through overlapping similarities.
mental_models.details: Instead of a strict definition, instances are connected by overlapping and criss-crossing similarities, much like the resemblance between members of a family. For example, the concept of a 'game' encompasses board games, card games, and Olympic games without one common denominator.

mental_models.label: The Ladder Metaphor
mental_models.summary: Using a structured philosophical system to achieve a correct perspective, only to realize the system itself is nonsensical and must be discarded.
mental_models.details: Wittgenstein compared his own early propositions to a ladder that the reader must climb to see the world rightly, and then throw away once the insight is gained.

mental_models.label: Saying vs. Showing
mental_models.summary: The distinction between what can be stated as a fact and what can only be made manifest through the structure of language or action.
mental_models.details: Propositions can 'say' facts about the universe, but the logical structure of language, ethics, and the mystical cannot be stated; they make themselves manifest or are 'shown'.
mental_models.representative_quote: What can be shown cannot be said.

mental_models.label: The Fly Bottle
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for philosophical confusion, where the mind is trapped in pointless questing like a fly in a glass bottle.
mental_models.details: The goal of philosophy is not to build new theories, but simply to show the trapped mind the way out by dissolving the linguistic confusion.
mental_models.representative_quote: to show the fly the way out of the fly-bottle

mental_models.label: Logical Space
mental_models.summary: A conceptual framework where all possible states of affairs (atomic facts) exist.
mental_models.details: Objects and facts exist within a realm of all possible configurations. An object cannot be conceived outside the possibility of its connection with other things in this space.

heuristics.label: Pass Over in Silence
heuristics.summary: If something cannot be spoken about meaningfully within the logical limits of language, remain silent about it.
heuristics.representative_quote: Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.

heuristics.label: Look, Don't Think
heuristics.summary: Resist the urge to theorize or generalize; instead, observe how words are actually used in ordinary language.

heuristics.label: Look for the Use
heuristics.summary: Do not search for the meaning of a word as if it were an object; look at how it is used in practice.
heuristics.representative_quote: Don't look for the meaning as an object; look at the use.

heuristics.label: Renounce Influence
heuristics.summary: Make yourself independent of the world by renouncing any attempt to influence external happenings.
heuristics.representative_quote: I can only make myself independent of the world—and so in a certain sense master it—by renouncing any influence on happenings.

heuristics.label: Eliminate the Artificial
heuristics.summary: In art, architecture, and life, eliminate the dishonest, the unnecessary, and the merely decorative.
heuristics.representative_quote: In art it is hard to say anything that is as good as saying nothing.

heuristics.label: Occam's Razor for Signs
heuristics.summary: If a sign is not necessary, then it is meaningless.
heuristics.representative_quote: If a sign is not necessary then it is meaningless.

signature_quotes.label: Silence on the Unspeakable
signature_quotes.summary: The final proposition of the Tractatus, delineating the limits of language.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: What we cannot speak about we must pass over in silence.

signature_quotes.label: The World is the Case
signature_quotes.summary: The opening proposition of the Tractatus, establishing his metaphysical view.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The world is all that is the case.

signature_quotes.label: Limits of Language and World
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the boundaries of thought and expression.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The limits of my language mean the limits of my world.

signature_quotes.label: Meaning is Use
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining his later philosophy on how words derive meaning.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: For a large class of cases of the employment of the word 'meaning'—though not for all—this word can be explained in this way: the meaning of a word is its use in the language.

signature_quotes.label: Philosophy as Activity
signature_quotes.summary: Defining the proper role and nature of philosophical work.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Philosophy is not a theory but an activity.

signature_quotes.label: The Fly-Bottle
signature_quotes.summary: Describing his ultimate purpose in philosophy.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: To show the fly the way out of the fly-bottle.

signature_quotes.label: Senseless Propositions
signature_quotes.summary: Critiquing the history of philosophical discourse.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Most propositions and questions, that have been written about philosophical matters, are not false, but senseless.

signature_quotes.label: The Form of a Philosophical Problem
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the nature of philosophical perplexity.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: A philosophical problem has the form: 'I don't know my way about.'

anti_patterns.label: Attempting to Say What Can Only Be Shown
anti_patterns.summary: Trying to use language to express its own logical form, ethics, or things outside the bounds of logical facts.
anti_patterns.details: A picture can represent reality, but it cannot represent its own form of representation; it can only show it. Trying to say what can only be shown results in inescapable logical contradictions and nonsense.

anti_patterns.label: The Augustinian Picture of Language
anti_patterns.summary: Believing that every word has a meaning, and this meaning is the object for which the word stands.
anti_patterns.details: This oversimplifies language by treating all words as nouns or names, ignoring the vast multiplicity of word functions (commands, questions, exclamations) and misleading philosophers into false metaphysical speculations.

anti_patterns.label: Treating Philosophy as a Natural Science
anti_patterns.summary: Attempting to solve philosophical problems by producing theories or treating them as empirical questions.
anti_patterns.details: Philosophy stands above or below the natural sciences. Its purpose is not to produce true propositions about the world, but to clarify the logical structure of thoughts and dissolve conceptual confusions.

anti_patterns.label: The Craving for Generality
anti_patterns.summary: Seeking essential definitions based on sufficient and necessary conditions.
anti_patterns.details: This blinds us to the actual, diverse uses of words in practice, imposing artificial boundaries where there is only fluid 'family resemblance'. Escaping confusion requires constant attention to detail and particular examples.

anti_patterns.label: Believing in a Private Language
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming it is possible to have a language that refers only to immediate private sensations.
anti_patterns.details: Meaningful language requires the possibility of judging the correctness of its use, which necessitates public standards. If the only judge of correctness is the inventor, then whatever seems right is right, meaning we cannot talk about 'right' at all.

anti_patterns.label: Treating the World as Isolated Objects
anti_patterns.summary: Treating the world as a mere collection of isolated objects or things.
anti_patterns.details: Objects only have meaning and reality in their combination into atomic facts. The world is determined by facts, and by them being all the facts, not by a list of independent things.
